highlights: Ciompi Uprising When Florence’s lowest-paid textile workers seized power, and how betrayal and ambition brought their revolution to a  ...    Unrest had been fomenting in Florence, especially in the Santa Croce and Oltrarno neighbourhoods, before it broke out into what started, at least in the beginning, as a bloodless coup on June 24, 1378, which was led by the humblest of workers in the cloth industry, including day labourers, wool combers, dyers and those doing poorly paid jobs like the many shop boys employed in the trade. Known as the Ciompi, deriving from the French word compère (meaning “buddy” or  ...    powerful and wealthy wool guild, which meant they could not benefit from most of its considerable privileges and perks. The revolt was sparked by the election of Salvestro de’ Medici, a cousin of the banking branch of the family, who was nominated gonfalonier by popular acclaim on June 18, 1378. title: Piazza dei Ciompi and its stories - Leonardo da Vinci Art School
highlights: Piazza dei Ciompi is a small square in the heart of the Santa Croce neighborhood. Many buildings in this area date back  ...    dedicated? They were paid wool workers ("ciompare" means "to beat," referring to the beating of the wool fibers during processing). The production and trade of luxury fabrics was the basis of Florence's economic development, which, along with the banks, enriched the leading families, the so-called "popolo grasso" (fat people).In 1378, the Ciompi, who worked in unhealthy conditions, were exploited and had no political representation, and revolted. How it startedFlorence in the 14th century was governed by the "people," but this term didn't have the same meaning we give it today.
